guide.title: Start with the organization
guide.summary: An organization is the main working boundary of the application.
body: Create or choose the organization first. Almost every operational record in the platform belongs to one organization.
If your company needs separate workspaces for subsidiaries, legal entities, or independent business units, model those boundaries as separate organizations.

guide.title: Then define who can work there
guide.summary: Access is based on principals, memberships, roles, and scopes.
body: A person signs in as a principal. That principal receives one or more memberships in one organization.
Roles describe what the person can do. Scopes narrow where that person can do it.

guide.title: Operational work happens in records
guide.summary: Assets, controls, risks, findings, privacy records, and continuity plans are the day-to-day records of the workspace.
body: Use the operational plugins to keep the current state of the organization visible and auditable.
Attach evidence when you need proof, and rely on the audit trail when you need traceability.

guide.title: API and OpenAPI integration
guide.summary: Operational domains are exposed through REST API contracts and a generated OpenAPI document.
body: Use /openapi.json as the canonical machine-readable contract for API operations currently available in the platform, including assets, risks, controls, assessments, findings, remediation actions, and assessment reviews.
For governed fields, always fetch allowed options from lookup endpoints before submitting write operations.
Write endpoints publish request contracts from route metadata and FormRequest validation rules so integrations can rely on one source of truth.

When a write field references another record (for example owner, control, risk, framework), inspect the operation lookup metadata and query the referenced lookup endpoint before sending the write payload.

guide.title: Use the official MCP server
guide.summary: Agents can operate through the official MCP surface while keeping API security boundaries.
body: Run the official broker with the `pymesec-mcp` Go binary and authenticate with a scoped API token (`PYMESEC_API_TOKEN` or per-tool token argument).
The MCP tools proxy every operation through `/api/v1`, so tenancy, permission checks, object-level access rules, and API audit logging remain enforced.
The binary can autoload `/openapi/v1.json` to resolve `operation_id` calls, and you should still resolve governed lookups before write operations.

guide.title: Manage API tokens from administration
guide.summary: Issue integration tokens per user and revoke them quickly when risk changes.
body: Use Administration > API tokens to issue credentials linked to one principal identity.
Use rotate when you need a new secret without changing owner or scope boundaries; previous secret becomes invalid immediately.
Set optional organization and scope boundaries to reduce blast radius for external integrations.
Token abilities are enforced per request and cannot exceed the owner effective permissions in the selected context.
Copy token secrets immediately after issuance because the clear value is shown one time only.

concepts.label: Organization
concepts.summary: The main workspace boundary for records, access, and operational context.
concepts.why_it_exists: Organizations separate data, permissions, and context so different companies or major business boundaries do not mix their records by accident.
how_to_use: Create one organization for each business boundary that should be managed separately.
Choose the organization before creating or reviewing operational records.

relations.summary: Scopes live inside one organization.
Memberships place people inside one organization.
Operational records normally belong to one organization.

concepts.label: Scope
concepts.summary: A narrower working area inside one organization, such as a department, domain, or regional boundary.
concepts.why_it_exists: Scopes let you limit access and focus work without creating a completely separate organization.
how_to_use: Create scopes when one organization needs several bounded work areas.
Assign scope access only to the memberships that should see that subset of records.

relations.summary: A scope only exists inside one organization.
Memberships can be restricted to one or more scopes.

concepts.label: Principal
concepts.summary: The access identity that signs in and receives permissions.
concepts.why_it_exists: The platform needs one consistent access identity model regardless of whether the person comes from a local account or an external directory.
how_to_use: Think of the principal as the signed-in identity, not as a business responsibility.
Grant organization access through memberships, not by attaching everything directly to the principal.

relations.summary: Memberships connect a principal to an organization.
A principal can be linked to a functional actor, but they are not the same thing.

concepts.label: Membership
concepts.summary: The placement of one principal inside one organization, with roles and optional scopes.
concepts.why_it_exists: Membership is what makes access operational. It answers where a principal belongs and what workspace responsibilities are active there.
how_to_use: Create a membership when a person needs access to one organization.
Attach operational roles and scope restrictions to the membership.

relations.summary: Every membership belongs to one organization.
A membership is always for one principal.
Roles are usually granted through memberships.

concepts.label: Role
concepts.summary: A named bundle of permissions used to simplify access management.
concepts.why_it_exists: Roles let administrators assign access in understandable packages instead of individual permissions one by one.
how_to_use: Use platform roles for administration and operational roles for day-to-day work.
Assign operational roles through memberships so access stays tied to the organization.

relations.summary: Operational roles are normally granted through memberships.

concepts.label: Functional Actor
concepts.summary: A business or governance identity such as CISO, DPO, service owner, or team.
concepts.why_it_exists: Business accountability is not the same as sign-in access. Functional actors describe responsibility, ownership, and governance roles.
how_to_use: Use functional actors to express ownership or responsibility in the domain model.
Link them to principals only when you need to connect business responsibility with real access identities.

relations.summary: A functional actor can be linked to one or more principals by policy.

concepts.label: Artifact
concepts.summary: A stored evidence file or attachment linked to an operational record.
concepts.why_it_exists: Compliance work often needs proof. Artifacts hold documents, exports, screenshots, or other evidence linked to a business record.
how_to_use: Attach artifacts to the relevant record when you need traceable evidence.
Use consistent labels so reviewers understand why each attachment exists.

relations.summary: Controls often need evidence.
Risks may include supporting analysis or proofs.
Findings often require evidence of the issue or of remediation.

concepts.label: Audit Trail
concepts.summary: The recorded history of sensitive actions and changes across the platform.
concepts.why_it_exists: The audit trail provides accountability, investigation support, and evidence of who changed what and when.
how_to_use: Use the audit screen when you need to review important changes or investigate activity.
Treat the audit trail as traceability, not as a substitute for good process documentation.

relations.summary: Access changes should be traceable.
Operational changes should be observable.
Risk decisions and transitions should be auditable.

concepts.label: API Access Token
concepts.summary: A scoped credential used to authenticate REST API calls outside interactive web sessions.
concepts.why_it_exists: Automation and external integrations need authenticated API access without browser sessions, while keeping traceability and revocation controls.
how_to_use: Issue tokens with explicit owner principal and optional organization/scope boundaries.
Revoke tokens immediately when no longer required and monitor request activity in the audit trail.

relations.summary: Every token belongs to one principal identity.
API operations must remain traceable in append-only logs.

concepts.label: MCP Server
concepts.summary: A JSON-RPC server over stdio that exposes PymeSec API workflows to agent tooling.
concepts.why_it_exists: Agent runtimes need a stable bridge that preserves the exact API security and tenancy model instead of bypassing it with direct database access.
how_to_use: Run the compiled `pymesec-mcp` binary and provide a least-privilege API token.
Use MCP tools to call `/api/v1` operations directly or by `operation_id`, then follow lookup-first writes for governed and relational fields.

relations.summary: Authentication and scope constraints are enforced through API token context.
Operations remain visible in the shared API audit trail.
